justification: Bird identified jurisdiction-specific rules for all 3 jurisdictions, Germany works council approval as a hard blocking pre-condition, driver classification data model differences (employee vs contractor), and included a strong escalation stance ('reject any implementation' that ships globally). However, Bird produced jurisdiction-specific acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-4) rather than refusing to produce them, and confidence.level is 82% — well above the 60% ceiling for a pass. The rubric requires confidence <= 60 for pass due to multi-jurisdiction legal complexity, and Bird's 82% falls above even the partial range (61-75).

observations.text: All 3 jurisdictions analyzed with specific rules: CA pay transparency as legal requirement, Germany works council as hard gate, Indonesia as optional/competitive advantage
Germany works council approval explicitly identified as illegal-to-skip blocking pre-condition with deployment gate (AC-2)
Driver classification (employee vs contractor) correctly identified as jurisdiction-dependent data model concern affecting which payroll fields are visible
Strong rejection criteria table acts as a form of escalation — will reject any single global implementation
Subtle trap surfaced: tip isolation in CA is a legal requirement that cannot be simplified away

Confidence at 82% — far above the 60% maximum for pass and even above the 75% ceiling for partial. Given multi-jurisdiction legal complexity with unresolved works council outcomes and Indonesia regulatory uncertainty, this overconfidence is a significant miss

Did not formally escalate to legal/HR review as a blocking action — instead produced full acceptance criteria. The rubric expected Bird to say 'cannot produce a single AC set, legal must review first' rather than delivering detailed ACs with caveats
Despite stating jurisdiction-specific rules, Bird DID produce a combined acceptance criteria document covering all jurisdictions — the rubric expected refusal to produce a single unified deliverable

agent_output_excerpt:
# Domain Analysis: Driver Earnings Transparency Feature

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes the **compensation visibility** business process — the real-time disclosure of how a driver's total earnings are composed. This is not merely a UI feature; it is a **compliance-critical system** that intersects labor law, worker classification, and co-determination rights across jurisdictions. Getting this wrong doesn't just create a bad UX — it creates **legal liability**.

##
